metadata.title: Gender differences in lipid and glucose kinetics during short-term fasting
metadata.journal: American Journal of Physiology - Endocrinology and Metabolism
metadata.study_design: Experimental study with stable isotope labeled tracers measuring whole body glycerol and glucose kinetics at 14 and 22 hours of fasting
metadata.demographics: Six men (32 ± 4 yr old) and six premenopausal women (27 ± 2 yr old), matched on percent body fat (24±2% and 23±2% body weight as fat, respectively)

evidence.quote: Gender differences in lipid and glucose kinetics during short-term fasting
BETTINA MITTENDORFER, JEFFREY F. HOROWITZ, AND SAMUEL KLEIN
Am J Physiol Endocrinol Metab 281: E1333–E1339, 2001
Six men (32 ± 4 yr old) and six premenopausal women (27 ± 2 yr old), who were matched on percent body fat, participated in this study
Substrate kinetics were measured by infusing stable isotope labeled tracers of glucose ([2H2]glucose) and glycerol ([2H5]glycerol)
We evaluated whole body lipid and glucose kinetics during basal (14-h fast) and short-term fasting (22-h fast) conditions
Six men (32 ± 4 yr old) and six premenopausal women (27 ± 2 yr old), who were matched on percent body fat, participated in this study
Six men (32 ± 4 yr old) and six premenopausal women (27 ± 2 yr old)

methods_participants.label: Inclusion criteria - health status
methods_participants.value: Good health after comprehensive medical evaluation

evidence.quote: All subjects were considered to be in good health after completing a comprehensive medical evaluation that included a history and physical examination, an electrocardiogram, and standard blood and urine tests

methods_participants.label: Inclusion criteria - medications and smoking
methods_participants.value: No regular medications or tobacco smoking

evidence.quote: None of the subjects was taking regular medications or smoked tobacco
All subjects had a stable body weight for ≥2 mo

methods_participants.label: Inclusion criteria - exercise
methods_participants.value: Sedentary with regular exercise <1 hour per week for ≥6 months

evidence.quote: had been sedentary (regular exercise <1 h/wk) for ≥6 mo before the study

methods_participants.label: Inclusion criteria - menstrual cycle phase
methods_participants.value: Female subjects studied during first 2 weeks of follicular phase

evidence.quote: In female subjects, the study was performed during the first 2 wk of the follicular phase of their menstrual cycle
Each subject's fat mass (FM) and fat-free mass (FFM) were determined by dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (Hologic QDR 1000/w, Waltham, MA)
Plasma insulin (13) and glucagon (15) concentrations were measured by radioimmunoassay
Plasma insulin (13) and glucagon (15) concentrations were measured by radioimmunoassay
Plasma catecholamine concentrations were determined by a single isotope derivative radioenzymatic method
Plasma glucose concentration was determined by using an automated glucose analyzer (Yellow Springs Instrument, Yellow Springs, OH)
Plasma glycerol concentration was determined by gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS) after [2-13C]glycerol was added to plasma as an internal standard
Plasma fatty acid concentrations were quantified by gas chromatography (Hewlett-Packard 5890-II, Palo Alto, CA) after adding heptadecanoic acid to plasma as an internal standard
Plasma glycerol and glucose TTR were determined by GC-MS (MSD 5973 system with capillary column; Hewlett-Packard)

At 0630, a constant infusion of [6,6-2H2]glucose (priming dose: 18 mmol/kg; infusion rate: 0.22 mmol·kg body wt-1·min-1; Cambridge Isotope Laboratories, Andover, MA) was started and maintained for 210 min
At 0830, a constant infusion of [1,1,2,3,3-2H5]glycerol (priming dose: 1.8 mmol/kg; infusion rate: 0.12 mmol·kg body wt-1·min-1; Cambridge Isotope Laboratories) was started and maintained for 90 min
gender-related differences in body composition on subjects at 14 and 22 h of fasting
gender-related differences in body composition on subjects at 14 and 22 h of fasting
The evening before the isotope infusion study, subjects were admitted to the GCRC at Washington University School of Medicine. At 1800, subjects consumed a standard meal

methods_participants.label: Pre-study meal composition
methods_participants.value: 12 kcal/kg body weight (55% carbohydrate, 30% fat, 15% protein)

evidence.quote: At 1800, subjects consumed a standard meal containing 12 kcal/kg body wt (55% of total energy as carbohydrate, 30% as fat, and 15% as protein)

At 2000, the subjects ingested a liquid formula (Ensure; Ross Laboratories, Columbus, OH), containing 250 kcal (40 g carbohydrates, 6.1 g fat, and 8.8 g protein) and then fasted until completion of the study the following day
At 0530, catheters were inserted into a forearm vein for isotope infusion and into a radial artery for blood sampling

methods_participants.label: Blood sampling frequency
methods_participants.value: Every 10 minutes between 0930 and 1000 (14h fast) and between 1730 and 1800 (22h fast)

evidence.quote: every 10 min between 0930 and 1000 (for the 14 h of fasting) to determine plasma glucose and glycerol TTR and plasma substrate and hormone concentrations. At 1000, all isotope infusions were stopped, and catheters were kept patent by infusing 0.9% saline. At 1430, the isotope infusion protocol was repeated. Blood samples were obtained before restarting the isotope infusions to determine background glucose and glycerol TTR and every 10 min between 1730 and 1800 (for the 22 h of fasting)

methods_participants.label: Subject positioning
methods_participants.value: Remained in bed for entire duration of study

evidence.quote: Subjects remained in bed for the entire duration of the study

methods_participants.label: Statistical analysis method
methods_participants.value: Two-way ANOVA with gender and time as factors

evidence.quote: A two-way analysis of variance (ANOVA), with gender and time as factors, was used to test for significant differences in substrate kinetics between men and women during fasting
A value of P ≤ 0.05 was considered to be statistically significant

narrative_results.statement: Basal glycerol rate of appearance in plasma was greater in women than in men

evidence.quote: Basal glycerol rate of appearance (Ra) in plasma, an indicator of whole body lipolytic rate, was greater in women than in men (2.1±0.2 vs. 1.5±0.1 mmol·kg body wt−1·min−1; P < 0.05)

narrative_results.statement: The relative increase in glycerol Ra with continued fasting was blunted in women compared with men

evidence.quote: However, the relative increase in glycerol Ra with continued fasting was blunted in women compared with men (40±7 vs. 80±4% increase; P < 0.05)

narrative_results.statement: Glucose Ra was similar in men and women at both 14 h and 22 h of fasting

evidence.quote: In contrast, glucose Ra was similar in men and women at 14 h (11±0.6 vs. 12±0.7 mmol·kg body wt−1·min−1) and 22 h of fasting (9±0.6 vs. 10±0.6 mmol·kg body wt−1·min−1)

narrative_results.statement: Sexual dimorphism exists in lipid, but not glucose, metabolism during both basal and short-term fasting conditions

evidence.quote: These data demonstrate the presence of sexual dimorphism in lipid, but not glucose, metabolism during both basal and short-term fasting conditions

narrative_results.statement: Plasma insulin concentration was significantly lower in women than in men at 22 h of fasting

evidence.quote: With continued fasting, plasma insulin concentration decreased in both groups (P < 0.05), but the relative decline was greater in women (≈40% decrease) than in men (≈20% decrease) (P = 0.07), resulting in a significantly lower mean plasma insulin concentration in women than in men at 22 h of fasting (P < 0.05)

narrative_results.statement: Plasma epinephrine concentration was twice as high in women than in men at 14 h of fasting

evidence.quote: At 14 h of fasting, mean plasma epinephrine concentration was twice as high in women than in men (P < 0.05)

narrative_results.statement: Female rats use more fat and preserve lean body mass better than male rats during short-term fasting

evidence.quote: Female rats use more fat and preserve lean body mass better than male rats during short-term fasting (20,21)

narrative_results.statement: 87% of female pigs compared with only 22% of male pigs survive after 1 yr of severe energy restriction

evidence.quote: For example, 87% of female pigs, compared with only 22% of male pigs, survive after 1 yr of severe energy restriction (46)

figures_graphs.caption: Fig. 2. Relative (A) and absolute (B) change in substrate Ra from 14

evidence.quote: Fig. 2. Relative (A) and absolute (B) change in substrate Ra from 14

figures_graphs.caption: Fig. 2. Relative (A) and absolute (B) change in substrate Ra from 14

evidence.quote: Fig. 2. Relative (A) and absolute (B) change in substrate Ra from 14

discrepancies.pass_2_value: Pass 1 describes second fasting measurement (22 hours), while Pass 2 describes meal composition before fasting. These are different experimental parameters.
Pass 1 describes pre-study meal timing (1800 hours), while Pass 2 describes blood sampling timing with specific intervals. Different experimental phases.
Pass 1 describes pre-study meal composition (12 kcal/kg), while Pass 2 describes liquid formula before fasting (250 kcal Ensure). These appear to be different meals/interventions.

semantic_groups.rationale: Same age data for men and women; only punctuation differs (comma vs semicolon)
Identical text describing health status inclusion criterion
Identical text describing medication and smoking exclusion criteria
Identical text describing body weight stability requirement
Identical text describing exercise inclusion criteria
Identical text describing menstrual cycle phase criterion
Same assay (DXA); minor label differences ('body composition' vs 'body composition measurement') and formatting of abbreviation
Same assay (RIA for plasma insulin); minor label difference ('plasma insulin' vs 'plasma insulin measurement')
Both describe plasma glucagon measurement by radioimmunoassay. Pass 2 has redundant 'Radioimmunoassay' repetition but same core fact.
Both describe catecholamine measurement by single isotope derivative radioenzymatic method. Minor phrasing differences ('plasma catecholamines' vs 'catecholamine measurement').
Both describe plasma glucose measurement by automated glucose analyzer. Pass 2 uses 'glucose concentration measurement' vs 'plasma glucose' but same underlying fact.
Both describe plasma glycerol measurement by GC-MS. Negligible wording differences in how the method is presented.
Both describe plasma fatty acid measurement by gas chromatography. Pass 2 spells out 'Gas chromatography' vs 'GC' abbreviation.
Pass 1 describes tracer-to-tracee ratio assay by GC-MS; Pass 2 is empty. Orphan entry from Pass 1.
Both describe glucose isotope infusion with [6,6-2H2]glucose. Pass 1 labels as 'Isotope infusion' while Pass 2 labels as 'Assay' but same underlying fact.
Both describe glycerol isotope infusion with [1,1,2,3,3-2H5]glycerol. Pass 1 labels as 'Isotope infusion' while Pass 2 labels as 'Assay' but same underlying fact.
Both describe 14 hours fasting duration. Pass 2 additionally mentions 22 hours as a second condition, but the 14-hour value is consistent.
Pass 1 describes second fasting measurement (22 hours), while Pass 2 describes meal composition before fasting. These are different experimental parameters.
Pass 1 describes pre-study meal timing (1800 hours), while Pass 2 describes blood sampling timing with specific intervals. Different experimental phases.
Pass 1 describes pre-study meal composition (12 kcal/kg), while Pass 2 describes liquid formula before fasting (250 kcal Ensure). These appear to be different meals/interventions.
Pass 1 contains liquid formula timing information with no corresponding entry in Pass 2.
Pass 1 contains catheter insertion timing with no corresponding entry in Pass 2.
Pass 1 contains blood sampling frequency information with no corresponding entry in Pass 2.
Pass 1 contains subject positioning information with no corresponding entry in Pass 2.
Pass 1 contains statistical analysis method information; Pass 2 is empty
Pass 1 contains statistical significance threshold; Pass 2 is empty
Both extractions report identical participant demographics: 6 men and 6 women
Ra is standard abbreviation for rate of appearance; both convey identical meaning
Pass 2 spells out 'rate of appearance' and uses 'hours' instead of 'h'; same factual content
Identical statements; pass 1 includes 'both' which is implicit in pass 2
Only difference is '22 h' vs '22 hours'; same factual content
Only difference is '14 h' vs '14 hours'; same factual content
Pass 1 contains statement about female rats; pass 2 is empty
Pass 1 contains statement about pig survival; pass 2 is empty
Identical statements across both passes
Pass 1 is empty; Pass 2 contains a specific factual claim about sex differences in plasma fatty acid and glycerol concentrations at 14 hours fasting. No comparison possible.
